explicit_text: I was involved in the response to the attack on the Pentagon on September 11, 2001. Flying over the site, I initially couldn't believe the small size of the hole in the building, which made us think it might have been a truck bomb rather than an aircraft. As we made more passes, the extent of the damage and the aircraft's penetration into the building became clear. The scene was chaotic but orderly, with many military and civilian personnel volunteering to help. We also used infrared technology to check for hot spots on the roof to assist firefighting efforts.

explicit_text: After the Pentagon attack, we took the chief of the Arlington County Fire Department up in our helicopter to get an overview of the scene. The department was heavily involved in the emergency response, and their chief's perspective from above helped coordinate efforts on the ground. We worked closely with them during the initial hours, especially as they managed triage and patient transport. Their professionalism was crucial in managing the aftermath of the crash and fire.

explicit_text: The FBI was a key player in the response after the Pentagon attack. Our helicopter crew was tasked with downlinking video footage to assist their investigation. We also flew FBI agents out to Dulles International Airport, since one of the hijacked planes had originated there. The FBI coordinated closely with us and other agencies to secure the area and gather intelligence during those critical days.

explicit_text: Following the Pentagon attack, our helicopter crews began regular patrols for the Secret Service, flying around the White House and other restricted zones in Washington, D.C. We took Secret Service personnel up every hour to help secure the area and maintain a visible presence. This was part of a heightened security posture that lasted for weeks after the attacks. The Secret Service relied on our aviation support to help protect key government sites during that tense period.

Our role was to assist in transporting officials quickly and safely between key sites.

explicit_text: MEDSTAR was on mass casualty alert following the Pentagon attack, prepared to receive numerous burn victims. I transported a shooting victim to MEDSTAR shortly after the attack, and the trauma team there was massive—about 60 doctors and nurses ready for incoming patients. Although they expected more burn victims, they treated all patients with urgency and care. MEDSTAR's readiness and response were vital in managing the medical crisis that day.

explicit_text: Interstate 295 runs near the Pentagon and was part of the area affected by the attack. We noticed light posts along the road near the heliport that had been knocked down by the aircraft. The proximity of the highway to the crash site highlighted the danger and destruction caused by the impact. Emergency vehicles used routes near I-295 to respond quickly to the scene and transport patients.

explicit_text: I was directly involved in the emergency response to the September 11 attacks, particularly at the Pentagon. The day was surreal, with massive fire, smoke, and devastation unlike anything I'd seen before. Our helicopter crew provided aerial surveillance, medical evacuation readiness, and support to multiple agencies including the FBI and Secret Service. The response lasted for weeks, with ongoing patrols and heightened security measures in the Washington, D.C. area. It was a day none of us will ever forget, though it's difficult to relive.

explicit_text: During the confusion of the September 11 attacks, there was a report of a plane inbound to Washington that never arrived, which we later understood was the plane that crashed in Pennsylvania. This information helped clarify the situation as the events unfolded. Pennsylvania became significant as the site of one of the hijacked planes' crash, linking it to the broader attack. It was part of the complex and rapidly changing information we had to process that day.
